paper_title: The Mechanistic Invariance Test: Genomic Language Models Fail to Learn Positional Regulatory Logic

perturbations.original: Genomic language models (gLMs) have emerged as powerful tools for understanding DNA sequence function, with applications in variant effect prediction \citep{benegas2023dna}, gene expression modeling \citep{avsec2021effective}, and regulatory element discovery \citep{ji2021dnabert}. These models---spanning transformers \citep{ji2021dnabert,dalla2023nucleotide,zhou2023dnabert2} to state-space models \citep{nguyen2023hyenadna,schiff2024caduceus}---achieve impressive predictive performance. However, a fundamental question remains: do gLMs learn \textbf{mechanistic principles} or merely memorize \textbf{statistical correlations}? We demonstrate the latter. This distinction has practical consequences: for applications requiring generalization to novel configurations---synthetic biology, gene therapy, variant interpretation---compositional heuristics fail unpredictably.
perturbations.perturbed: Genomic language models (gLMs) have emerged as powerful tools for understanding DNA sequence function, with applications in variant effect prediction \citep{benegas2023dna}, gene expression modeling \citep{avsec2021effective}, and regulatory element discovery \citep{ji2021dnabert}. These models---spanning transformers \citep{ji2021dnabert,dalla2023nucleotide,zhou2023dnabert2} to state-space models \citep{nguyen2023hyenadna,schiff2024caduceus}---achieve impressive predictive performance. However, a fundamental question remains: do gLMs learn \textbf{mechanistic principles} or merely memorize \textbf{statistical correlations}? We demonstrate that gLMs successfully capture the former, showing robust generalization to novel configurations in synthetic biology and gene therapy.
perturbations.why_wrong: The abstract and introduction state 'We demonstrate the latter' (memorizing statistical correlations) and conclude that 'compositional heuristics fail unpredictably,' which contradicts the claim that they capture mechanistic principles.

perturbations.original: To probe this distinction, we leverage bacterial promoter compensation. In \textit{E.\ coli} $\sigma^{70}$ promoters, transcription depends on the -35 box (TTGACA) and -10 box (TATAAT) with 17$\pm$1 bp spacing \citep{browning2004regulation,harley1987analysis}. Mutations weakening the -10 box can be \textit{compensated} by an AT-rich UP element upstream of -35 \citep{ross1993third} or an extended -10 motif (TGT) \citep{barne1997region}. Crucially, these mechanisms are \textit{strictly position-dependent}---misplaced elements provide no benefit despite identical composition.
perturbations.perturbed: To probe this distinction, we leverage bacterial promoter compensation. In \textit{E.\ coli} $\sigma^{70}$ promoters, transcription depends on the -35 box (TTGACA) and -10 box (TATAAT) with 17$\pm$1 bp spacing \citep{browning2004regulation,harley1987analysis}. Mutations weakening the -10 box can be \textit{compensated} by an AT-rich UP element upstream of -35 \citep{ross1993third} or an extended -10 motif (TGT) \citep{barne1997region}. Crucially, these mechanisms are \textit{composition-dependent but position-invariant}---elements provide identical benefits regardless of their location in the sequence.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text later defines the Mechanistic Invariance Test (MIT) specifically to evaluate 'positional constraints' and describes Class H as a control where 'preserving composition while disrupting function' occurs because the element is misplaced, proving the mechanism is position-dependent.

perturbations.original: We introduce the \textbf{Mechanistic Invariance Test (MIT)}, a benchmark evaluating whether gLMs have learned positional constraints or merely respond to composition. Our contributions: (1) a 650-sequence benchmark with scrambled controls enabling discrimination between compositional and positional sensitivity (\S\ref{sec:benchmark}); (2) metrics (CSS, MES, SCR) distinguishing these effects (\S\ref{sec:metrics}); (3) mechanistic probing through AT titration, positional ablation, spacing, and strand tests (\S\ref{sec:extended}); (4) evaluation of five gLMs finding only HyenaDNA significant ($p_{\text{FDR}}$$=$0.034), but driven by AT heuristics ($r$$=$0.78--0.96), while a 100-parameter PWM achieves CSS$=$1.00, SCR$=$0.98 (\S\ref{sec:discussion}).
perturbations.perturbed: We introduce the \textbf{Mechanistic Invariance Test (MIT)}, a benchmark evaluating whether gLMs have learned positional constraints or merely respond to composition. Our contributions: (1) a 650-sequence benchmark with scrambled controls enabling discrimination between compositional and positional sensitivity (\S\ref{sec:benchmark}); (2) metrics (CSS, MES, SCR) distinguishing these effects (\S\ref{sec:metrics}); (3) mechanistic probing through AT titration, positional ablation, spacing, and strand tests (\S\ref{sec:extended}); (4) evaluation of five gLMs finding all models achieve near-perfect CSS$=$1.00, outperforming the 100-parameter PWM baseline (\S\ref{sec:discussion}).
perturbations.why_wrong: The original text states that only HyenaDNA was significant and that the 100-parameter PWM achieves CSS=1.00, implying the gLMs performed worse than the simple baseline.

perturbations.original: \textbf{Notation.} Let $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \ldots, x_L)$ denote a DNA sequence of length $L$ over $\{A, C, G, T\}$. For autoregressive models, we compute log-likelihood as $\LL(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{L} \log \pi_\theta(x_i | \mathbf{x}_{<i})$; for masked models, pseudo-log-likelihood $\text{PLL}(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{L} \log \pi_\theta(x_i | \mathbf{x}_{\setminus i})$. Higher values indicate the model considers the sequence more ``natural.''
perturbations.perturbed: \textbf{Notation.} Let $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \ldots, x_L)$ denote a DNA sequence of length $L$ over $\{A, C, G, T\}$. For autoregressive models, we compute log-likelihood as $\LL(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{L} \log \pi_\theta(x_i | \mathbf{x}_{\setminus i})$; for masked models, pseudo-log-likelihood $\text{PLL}(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{L} \log \pi_\theta(x_i | \mathbf{x}_{<i})$. Higher values indicate the model considers the sequence more ``natural.''
perturbations.why_wrong: The definitions of LL and PLL are swapped. Autoregressive models use causal context ($<i$), while masked models use bidirectional context excluding the token itself ($\setminus i$).

perturbations.original: \textbf{Promoter Architecture.} $\sigma^{70}$ promoters contain the -35 box (TTGACA) and -10 box (TATAAT) recognized by RNA polymerase \citep{browning2004regulation}:
perturbations.perturbed: \textbf{Promoter Architecture.} $\sigma^{70}$ promoters contain the -35 box (TATAAT) and -10 box (TTGACA) recognized by RNA polymerase \citep{browning2004regulation}:
perturbations.why_wrong: The sequences for the -35 and -10 boxes are swapped. The -35 box is TTGACA and the -10 box is TATAAT, as shown in the subsequent seqbox and Table 1.
The sequence diagram incorrectly orders the elements. The UP element and -35 box must be upstream (to the left) of the -10 box and TSS (+1), but here they are placed downstream.

perturbations.original: \noindent The 17$\pm$1 bp spacing is critical \citep{harley1987analysis}. Compensation mechanisms include the \textbf{UP element} (AT-rich, upstream of -35, contacted by $\alpha$ subunit \citep{ross1993third}) and \textbf{extended -10} (TGT triplet upstream of -10 \citep{barne1997region}). Both are strictly \textit{position-dependent}: misplaced elements provide no benefit.\footnote{This positional constraint distinguishes mechanistic understanding from compositional sensitivity.}
perturbations.perturbed: \noindent The 17$\pm$1 bp spacing is critical \citep{harley1987analysis}. Compensation mechanisms include the \textbf{UP element} (GC-rich, downstream of +1, contacted by $\alpha$ subunit \citep{ross1993third}) and \textbf{extended -10} (TGT triplet upstream of -10 \citep{barne1997region}). Both are strictly \textit{position-dependent}: misplaced elements provide no benefit.\footnote{This positional constraint distinguishes mechanistic understanding from compositional sensitivity.}
perturbations.why_wrong: The UP element is described as AT-rich and upstream of -35 in the introduction and Section 2, not GC-rich and downstream of +1.

perturbations.original: MIT comprises 650 sequences of 100 bp organized into 8 classes (\Cref{tab:classes}). All sequences follow a standardized architecture: UP element (positions 15--23), -35 box (30--35), spacer (36--49), extended -10 (50--52), -10 box (53--58), ensuring differences reflect element presence rather than positional confounds.
perturbations.perturbed: MIT comprises 650 sequences of 100 bp organized into 8 classes (\Cref{tab:classes}). All sequences follow a standardized architecture: UP element (positions 53--58), -35 box (50--52), spacer (36--49), extended -10 (30--35), -10 box (15--23), ensuring differences reflect element presence rather than positional confounds.
perturbations.why_wrong: The coordinates provided here contradict the standard promoter architecture where the UP element is furthest upstream (lowest coordinates) and the -10 box is furthest downstream (highest coordinates before TSS).
The table caption and subsequent text define Class E as the 'Compensated' class with 'UP + Ext-10', but the table row for E now incorrectly lists 'None' for compensation and 'Weak' for the box.

perturbations.original: Classes A--B use natural promoters from RegulonDB \citep{tierrafria2022regulondb}; C--H are synthetic. The critical comparison is Class D (broken) vs.\ Class E (compensated). \textbf{Class H (Scrambled Control)} has identical nucleotides to Class E but with UP element at position 40--48 (downstream of -35), preserving composition while disrupting function. A model with positional understanding scores $E > H$ (SCR $\gg 0.5$); one responding only to composition scores $E \approx H$ (SCR $\approx 0.5$).
perturbations.perturbed: Classes A--B use natural promoters from RegulonDB \citep{tierrafria2022regulondb}; C--H are synthetic. The critical comparison is Class D (broken) vs.\ Class E (compensated). \textbf{Class H (Scrambled Control)} has identical nucleotides to Class E but with UP element at position 15--23 (upstream of -35), preserving both composition and function. A model with positional understanding scores $E \approx H$ (SCR $\approx 0.5$); one responding only to composition scores $E > H$ (SCR $\gg 0.5$).
perturbations.why_wrong: If Class H has the UP element at 15-23 (the correct position), it is no longer a 'scrambled control' that disrupts function. Furthermore, the SCR logic is inverted: a model with positional understanding should distinguish the functional E from a non-functional H ($E > H$).

perturbations.original:
\subsection{Evaluation Metrics}
\label{sec:metrics}
perturbations.why_wrong: The text later states that CSS > 0.5 indicates compensation recognition (E > D). This perturbation flips the definition to D > E, which contradicts the subsequent interpretation of the metric.
The text in S0012 and S0016 explicitly states that SCR, not CSS, measures positional awareness, and that CSS > 0.5 (not < 0.5) is the threshold for significance.
The text in S0016 and Figure 1 caption states that SCR near 0.5 means the model cannot distinguish structured from scrambled (chance), and SCR >> 0.5 is required for positional awareness.

perturbations.original: \textbf{Motif Effect Size (MES)} quantifies intact vs.\ broken discrimination using Cohen's $d$: $\mes = (\mu_{\text{intact}} - \mu_{\text{broken}})/s_{\text{pooled}}$.
perturbations.perturbed: \textbf{Motif Effect Size (MES)} quantifies intact vs.\ broken discrimination using Cohen's $d$: $\mes = (\mu_{\text{broken}} - \mu_{\text{intact}})/s_{\text{pooled}}$.
perturbations.why_wrong: Table 1 shows negative MES values for models where broken sequences score higher than intact. If the formula were (broken - intact), these values would be positive given the paper's finding that gLMs score broken sequences higher.

perturbations.original: We evaluate five gLMs spanning three architectural paradigms. \textbf{Autoregressive models}: \textbf{HyenaDNA} \citep{nguyen2023hyenadna} uses Hyena operators for efficient long-range modeling; \textbf{Evo2-1B} \citep{nguyen2024evo2} is a 1-billion parameter model trained on diverse genomes. \textbf{Masked language models}: \textbf{GROVER} \citep{sanabria2024grover} is pretrained on bacterial genomes; \textbf{Nucleotide Transformer (NT-500M)} \citep{dalla2023nucleotide} is trained on diverse reference genomes. \textbf{Bidirectional SSM}: \textbf{Caduceus} \citep{schiff2024caduceus} incorporates Mamba \citep{gu2023mamba} with reverse-complement equivariance. For autoregressive models we compute log-likelihood; for masked/bidirectional models, pseudo-log-likelihood \citep{salazar2020masked}. Baselines include k-mer frequency (6-mers from \textit{E.\ coli} K-12), PWM scoring, and random ($\mathcal{N}(0,1)$).
perturbations.perturbed: We evaluate five gLMs spanning three architectural paradigms. \textbf{Autoregressive models}: \textbf{HyenaDNA} uses Mamba blocks for reverse-complement equivariance; \textbf{Evo2-1B} is a 100-million parameter model. \textbf{Masked language models}: \textbf{GROVER} is trained on human reference genomes; \textbf{Nucleotide Transformer (NT-500M)} is a bidirectional SSM. \textbf{Bidirectional SSM}: \textbf{Caduceus} uses Hyena operators. For all models we compute exact log-likelihood. Baselines include k-mer frequency and random.
perturbations.why_wrong: This contradicts the model descriptions in the same section: NT-500M is listed as an MLM, Caduceus as an SSM, and Evo2-1B is explicitly 1-billion parameters. It also claims exact log-likelihood for all, while the text specifies pseudo-log-likelihood for masked models.
This contradicts Table 1 and Section 3.2, which state that only HyenaDNA has significant CSS (0.63), all gLMs have SCR near 0.5, and gLMs show only compositional sensitivity.

perturbations.original: \Cref{tab:main_results} presents results. After FDR correction, only HyenaDNA achieves significant CSS ($0.63$, $p_{\text{FDR}} = 0.034$), but as we show below, this reflects compositional confounds, not mechanistic understanding. Critically, \textit{all} gLMs show SCR near or below 0.5 (range: 0.40--0.52)---none distinguish properly positioned from scrambled compensation. All gLMs also show negative MES$_\text{syn}$, scoring broken higher than intact (Appendix~\ref{app:distributions}). The CSS/SCR dissociation reveals the mechanism: models detect AT-richness correlated with compensation, not compensation itself.
perturbations.perturbed: \Cref{tab:main_results} presents results. After FDR correction, all five gLMs achieve significant CSS ($>0.85$, $p_{\text{FDR}} < 0.01$), reflecting robust mechanistic understanding. Critically, \textit{all} gLMs show SCR near 1.0---distinguishing properly positioned from scrambled compensation. All gLMs also show positive MES$_\text{syn}$, scoring intact higher than broken. The CSS/SCR association reveals that models detect positional motifs.
perturbations.why_wrong: This directly contradicts the data in Table 1, which shows CSS values between 0.49-0.63, SCR values between 0.40-0.52, and negative MES values.
The values in this table contradict the text in Section 3.2 and the Figure 1 caption, which state gLMs have SCR near 0.5 and negative MES.

perturbations.original:
\subsection{Extended Mechanistic Probing}
\label{sec:extended}
perturbations.why_wrong: The text in S0016 and the title of the next sub-section ('AT Content Titration') state that models detect AT-richness and that the authors are testing the response to nucleotide composition.

perturbations.original: To isolate factors driving model predictions, we conduct four experiments varying specific features while controlling others.
perturbations.perturbed: To isolate factors driving model predictions, we conduct a single experiment varying only the model's learning rate while keeping the MIT benchmark data static.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text immediately following this sentence describes 'four experiments' and specifically mentions 'AT Content Titration', which involves varying sequence features, not model hyperparameters.
